serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g (one bowl or cup), as typically served in Singaporean dessert shops.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component ingredients: mango (USDA FoodData Central #169910), sago pearls (USDA FoodData Central #168878), coconut milk (USDA FoodData Central #169919), pomelo (USDA FoodData Central #174673); weighted composite per typical recipe proportions (mango ~35%, coconut milk ~30%, sago ~20%, pomelo ~15%).
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for mango pomelo sago exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of the four primary ingredients using USDA SR Legacy data. Proportions: mango 35%, coconut milk 30%, sago pearls 20%, pomelo 15%. Sugar addition estimated at ~5 g per 100 g of final product based on typical recipe references. Values are approximate and should be validated against laboratory analysis.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI based on analogous foods: mango GI ~51 (Atkinson FS et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); sago GI ~70 (Foster-Powell K et al., 2002, Am J Clin Nutr); coconut milk GI ~41 (estimated low); pomelo GI ~25 (low GI citrus). Weighted composite GI ~55 based on carbohydrate contribution of each ingredient.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed journals (Diabetes Care; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mango pomelo sago as a composite dish. GI estimated at 55 (low) using a carbohydrate-weighted average of component GI values sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature: mango GI 51 (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:S281-S283), sago GI 70 (Foster-Powell et al., 2002, Am J Clin Nutr 76:5-56), coconut milk GI ~41 (low GI fat-rich food), pomelo GI ~25 (low GI citrus). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (55 × 47.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 26.1. Available carbohydrate per 100g is 15.8g (total carbohydrate 16.5g minus dietary fibre 0.7g).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~24 mg per 300 g serving); potassium from mango and pomelo may support blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Mango pomelo sago is low in sodium (~8 mg per 100 g) and provides potassium (~130 mg per 100 g) from fruit components, making it generally suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed in moderate portions; however, added sugar content should be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) but high glycemic load per serving (~26.1) due to significant available carbohydrates from sago pearls, mango, and added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume mango pomelo sago in small portions due to its low GI (~55) but high glycemic load per standard serving (~26.1), which can still cause a meaningful postprandial blood glucose rise; pairing with a protein or fibre source may help blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat content (~4.2 g per 100 g) from coconut milk; low cholesterol and low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from coconut milk (~4.2 g per 100 g, predominantly medium-chain triglycerides) warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease, although the dish is cholesterol-free and low in sodium; limiting portion size to half a standard bowl is advisable for those with elevated LDL cholesterol.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango Pomelo Sago (杨枝甘露, Yang Zhi Gan Lu) is a classic Cantonese chilled dessert originating from Hong Kong and widely popular across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish consists of a creamy, lightly sweetened base made from blended fresh mango and full-fat coconut milk, enriched with cooked translucent sago pearls that provide a characteristic chewy, tapioca-like texture. The dessert is finished with generous chunks of fresh ripe mango and segments of red-fleshed pomelo, which contribute a refreshing citrus tartness that balances the richness of the coconut milk. In Singapore, premium versions such as the one served at RUXU 入续 糖水铺与包 use seasonal Tai Nong mangoes, prized for their intense sweetness and low fibre, and 100% pure coconut milk for a richer mouthfeel. The dessert is served cold and is considered a quintessential summer or warm-weather treat. Nutritionally, mango pomelo sago provides moderate energy (~112 kcal per 100 g), predominantly from carbohydrates (sago, mango sugars) and fat (coconut milk saturated fat). It is a source of vitamin C (~14 mg per 100 g) and vitamin A (~28 mcg RAE per 100 g) from the mango and pomelo. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal. Clinically, its low glycemic index (~55) but high glycemic load per standard 300 g serving (~26.1) mean that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume it in small portions. The saturated fat from coconut milk warrants moderation for those with cardiovascular disease. Sodium is low, making it generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for mango pomelo sago exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional nutrition database. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy data for the four primary ingredients (mango, coconut milk, sago pearls, pomelo). GI is estimated from analogous component GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible but has not been validated against laboratory analysis of the actual dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted ingredient composite (mango 35%, coconut milk 30%, sago 20%, pomelo 15%) using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data; no direct laboratory analysis of this dish is available in approved sources. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): coconut milk and fresh fruit ingredients contain no industrially produced trans fats; naturally occurring trans fats in coconut are negligible and reported as 0 in USDA data. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): all ingredients are plant-based; USDA data confirms 0 mg cholesterol for mango, pomelo, sago, and coconut milk. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): this is an entirely plant-based dessert with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is not present in plant foods. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): no vitamin D is present in the plant-based ingredients of this dessert; USDA data confirms 0 mcg for all components. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.2), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (130.0), calcium_mg (10.0), iron_mg (0.4), vitamin_c_mg (14.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (28.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.03), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.02), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.3), vitamin_b6_mg (0.08), folate_mcg_dfe (12.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.5), phosphorus_mg (22.0), magnesium_mg (12.0), zinc_mg (0.1), selenium_mcg (0.5), water_g (76.5) are all estimated from the weighted ingredient composite using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data. 7. GI estimated at 55 (low) using carbohydrate-weighted average of component GI values from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature; glycemic load per 300 g serving = (55 × 47.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 26.1. 8. The food_name has been normalised from the promotional input name 'Top-Tier Fresh Mango Pomelo Sago' to the canonical English name 'Mango Pomelo Sago'. 9. This dish is classified as a Singaporean-served Chinese dessert; multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per the Singaporean dish multilingual requirement.
